questions.prompt: A 4.50 g sample of pure magnesium (molar mass 24.3 g/mol) contains approximately how many atoms of Mg?
questions.explanation: Moles of Mg = 4.50 g ÷ 24.3 g/mol ≈ 0.185 mol. Multiplying by Avogadro's number (6.02 × 10^23) yields about 1.11 × 10^23 atoms.

questions.prompt: A mass spectrum of element X shows two peaks: m/z = 63 at 69.2% abundance and m/z = 65 at 30.8% abundance. What is the average atomic mass of X, and which element is most consistent with this data?
questions.explanation: Average mass = (63)(0.692) + (65)(0.308) ≈ 43.6 + 20.0 = 63.6 amu, matching copper (Cu), which has two natural isotopes (Cu-63 and Cu-65) near these abundances.

questions.prompt: A photoelectron spectrum (PES) of an element shows peaks corresponding to binding energies of 1.1 × 10^8, 6.84 × 10^6, 3.67 × 10^6, 0.494 × 10^6, and 0.419 × 10^6 J/mol, with relative peak intensities of 2:2:6:2:1. Which element best matches this pattern?
questions.explanation: The 2:2:6:2:1 intensity pattern corresponds to 1s² 2s² 2p⁶ 3s² 3p¹ — 13 electrons, the configuration of aluminum. PES peak heights are proportional to the number of electrons in each subshell.

questions.prompt: Which of the following correctly ranks first ionization energy from lowest to highest?
questions.explanation: Ionization energy decreases down a group (K < Na) and generally increases across a period. Al has lower IE than Mg because removing Al's 3p electron leaves a stable 3s² configuration, breaking the strict left-to-right trend.

questions.prompt: Coulomb's Law predicts that the electrostatic attraction between a nucleus and a valence electron will be greatest in which atom?
questions.explanation: Coulombic attraction scales as Z_eff × q / r². Fluorine has a high effective nuclear charge and a small atomic radius among the choices, producing the strongest pull on a valence electron — explaining F's very high ionization energy and electronegativity.

questions.prompt: Which Lewis structure prediction is correct for the SO₂ molecule?
choices: Linear geometry with no lone pairs on sulfur and zero net dipole
Bent geometry (~119°) with one lone pair on sulfur, two equivalent resonance structures, and a net dipole
Trigonal planar geometry with three identical S=O bonds and zero dipole
Tetrahedral geometry around sulfur with four equivalent S–O bonds
questions.correctText: Bent geometry (~119°) with one lone pair on sulfur, two equivalent resonance structures, and a net dipole
questions.explanation: Sulfur in SO₂ has 3 electron domains (2 bonding + 1 lone pair) → bent geometry near 120°. Two equivalent resonance structures distribute the double bond, and the lone pair gives a net dipole moment (unlike linear CO₂).

questions.prompt: Which substance is expected to have the highest melting point?
questions.explanation: Ionic lattices like NaCl require breaking strong electrostatic Na⁺–Cl⁻ attractions throughout a 3-D crystal, giving very high melting points (~801°C). Molecular substances held by weaker IMFs melt at far lower temperatures.

questions.prompt: A characteristic property of metallic bonding that distinguishes metals from ionic and molecular solids is:
choices: Localized electron pairs shared between specific atoms
A 'sea of delocalized electrons' surrounding a lattice of cations, producing high electrical conductivity and malleability
Discrete molecules held together by London dispersion forces
Alternating cations and anions in a rigid 3-D lattice with no mobile charges
questions.correctText: A 'sea of delocalized electrons' surrounding a lattice of cations, producing high electrical conductivity and malleability
questions.explanation: In the electron-sea model, metal cations sit in a lattice while valence electrons move freely throughout. This delocalization explains electrical/thermal conductivity, luster, and ductility — properties that ionic and molecular solids lack.

questions.prompt: Which pair of molecules contains hydrogen bonding as a significant intermolecular force?
questions.explanation: Hydrogen bonding occurs when H is bonded directly to N, O, or F. Both NH₃ (N–H) and H₂O (O–H) satisfy this requirement — explaining water's elevated boiling point relative to other hydrides of Group 16.

questions.prompt: A 2.50 L rigid container holds 0.150 mol of an ideal gas at 27°C. What is the pressure of the gas? (R = 0.0821 L·atm/mol·K)
questions.explanation: Using PV = nRT: P = (0.150 mol)(0.0821 L·atm/mol·K)(300 K) / 2.50 L ≈ 1.48 atm. Remember to convert 27°C to 300 K.

questions.prompt: Under which conditions does a real gas deviate most significantly from ideal-gas behavior?
questions.explanation: Kinetic Molecular Theory assumes negligible intermolecular forces and negligible molecular volume. At low T (IMFs become significant) and high P (molecular volume is not negligible), these assumptions fail and real gases deviate from PV = nRT.

questions.prompt: Liquid ethanol has a higher vapor pressure than liquid water at the same temperature. The best explanation is that:
choices: Ethanol molecules are more massive than water molecules.
Water has a smaller molar mass than ethanol.
Ethanol has weaker net intermolecular forces (fewer hydrogen bonds per molecule) than water, so molecules escape the liquid more readily.
Ethanol contains an ionic bond that disrupts hydrogen bonding.
questions.correctText: Ethanol has weaker net intermolecular forces (fewer hydrogen bonds per molecule) than water, so molecules escape the liquid more readily.
questions.explanation: Water can hydrogen-bond through two O–H sites per molecule; ethanol has only one O–H site (plus a hydrophobic C₂H₅ tail). Weaker overall IMFs in ethanol mean a higher equilibrium vapor pressure at the same temperature.

questions.prompt: How many milliliters of a 12.0 M HCl stock solution must be diluted to prepare 250.0 mL of 0.500 M HCl?
questions.explanation: Using M₁V₁ = M₂V₂: V₁ = (0.500 M)(250.0 mL) / 12.0 M ≈ 10.4 mL. Dilute that volume to a final volume of 250.0 mL with water.

questions.prompt: When solid NaCl dissolves in water, the boiling point of the solution rises and the freezing point falls. This phenomenon is best classified as:
choices: A chemical reaction between Na⁺ and H₂O
A colligative property dependent on the number of dissolved solute particles (the van't Hoff factor i ≈ 2 for NaCl)
A redox process driven by water acting as an oxidizer
A nuclear effect tied to the isotopes of sodium
questions.correctText: A colligative property dependent on the number of dissolved solute particles (the van't Hoff factor i ≈ 2 for NaCl)
questions.explanation: Colligative properties depend only on solute particle count, not identity. ΔT_b = i·K_b·m and ΔT_f = i·K_f·m. NaCl dissociates into Na⁺ + Cl⁻ (i ≈ 2), so it produces about twice the boiling-point elevation of an equimolar non-electrolyte like glucose.

questions.prompt: In an electrolysis experiment, a current of 2.00 A flows through molten AlCl₃ for 1.00 hour. Using Faraday's law (F = 96,485 C/mol e⁻; molar mass Al = 27.0 g/mol), what mass of aluminum is deposited at the cathode?
Balance the equation: __ C₂H₆ + __ O₂ → __ CO₂ + __ H₂O. What are the coefficients (in order, smallest whole-number set)?
questions.explanation: Balancing combustion of ethane: 2 C₂H₆ supplies 4 C (→ 4 CO₂) and 12 H (→ 6 H₂O). O needed = 4(2) + 6(1) = 14 atoms → 7 O₂. Final: 2 C₂H₆ + 7 O₂ → 4 CO₂ + 6 H₂O.

questions.prompt: Consider the reaction: N₂(g) + 3 H₂(g) → 2 NH₃(g). If 4.0 mol N₂ reacts with 9.0 mol H₂, which reagent is limiting, and how many moles of NH₃ are produced?
questions.explanation: 4.0 mol N₂ would need 12.0 mol H₂ (3:1 ratio), but only 9.0 mol H₂ is available — so H₂ limits. 9.0 mol H₂ × (2 mol NH₃ / 3 mol H₂) = 6.0 mol NH₃. About 1.0 mol N₂ is left over.

questions.prompt: In the reaction Zn(s) + 2 HCl(aq) → ZnCl₂(aq) + H₂(g), the element that is oxidized and the element that is reduced are:
choices: Zn is reduced; H is oxidized
Zn is oxidized; H is reduced
Cl is oxidized; H is reduced
Zn is oxidized; Cl is reduced
questions.correctText: Zn is oxidized; H is reduced
questions.explanation: Zn(0) → Zn²⁺ + 2 e⁻ (loss of electrons = oxidation). 2 H⁺ + 2 e⁻ → H₂ (gain of electrons = reduction). Cl remains at −1 throughout and is a spectator ion.

questions.prompt: Balance the half-reaction for permanganate in acidic solution: MnO₄⁻ + H⁺ + e⁻ → Mn²⁺ + H₂O. What are the correct coefficients?
questions.explanation: Mn goes from +7 (in MnO₄⁻) to +2, gaining 5 e⁻. Four O atoms become 4 H₂O, requiring 8 H⁺ to balance hydrogen. Charge balance: left = −1 + 8 − 5 = +2; right = +2. Balanced.

questions.prompt: For the reaction A → products, the rate law is found to be rate = k[A]². If the concentration of A is doubled, the initial rate will:
questions.explanation: For a second-order reaction in [A], doubling [A] multiplies the rate by 2² = 4. Compare: zero-order (no change), first-order (×2), second-order (×4), third-order (×8).

questions.prompt: A first-order reaction has a half-life of 25.0 s. How long will it take for the reactant concentration to drop to 12.5% of its initial value?
questions.explanation: For first-order kinetics, half-life is constant. After one half-life: 50%. After two: 25%. After three: 12.5%. Three half-lives × 25.0 s = 75.0 s.

questions.prompt: The Arrhenius equation k = A·e^(−Ea/RT) predicts that:
choices: Rate constant decreases with increasing temperature.
Rate constant increases exponentially as temperature increases, because more molecules have enough kinetic energy to overcome the activation barrier Ea.
Activation energy is independent of temperature, but the pre-exponential factor decreases with T.
Catalysts raise Ea to make reactions slower.
questions.correctText: Rate constant increases exponentially as temperature increases, because more molecules have enough kinetic energy to overcome the activation barrier Ea.
questions.explanation: Raising T expands the high-energy tail of the Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution, so a greater fraction of collisions exceed Ea. This causes the exponential rise in k captured by the Arrhenius equation.

questions.prompt: For a proposed mechanism: Step 1 (slow): NO₂ + NO₂ → NO₃ + NO; Step 2 (fast): NO₃ + CO → NO₂ + CO₂. What is the predicted rate law, and what is the rate-determining step?
questions.explanation: The rate law is determined by the slow (rate-limiting) step. Step 1 is bimolecular in NO₂, giving rate = k[NO₂]². CO does not appear in the rate law because it reacts only in the fast subsequent step.

questions.prompt: How does a catalyst increase the rate of a chemical reaction?
choices: It increases the activation energy of the forward reaction only.
It provides an alternate reaction pathway with a lower activation energy, without altering ΔH or the equilibrium constant.
It shifts the equilibrium toward the products by changing K.
It increases the temperature of the reaction mixture.
questions.correctText: It provides an alternate reaction pathway with a lower activation energy, without altering ΔH or the equilibrium constant.
questions.explanation: A catalyst opens a lower-Ea route, accelerating both the forward and reverse reactions equally. Because ΔH and ΔG are state functions, the position of equilibrium (and K) is unchanged — only the rate of approach increases.

questions.prompt: A 50.0 g sample of water at 25.0°C absorbs 1,256 J of energy. Given the specific heat capacity of water is 4.184 J/(g·°C), what is the final temperature of the water?

In a coffee-cup calorimeter, 100.0 mL of 1.00 M HCl at 22.0°C is mixed with 100.0 mL of 1.00 M NaOH at 22.0°C. The final temperature is 28.7°C. Assuming the solution behaves like water (C = 4.184 J/g·°C, density = 1.00 g/mL), what is the approximate enthalpy of neutralization per mole of water formed?
questions.explanation: q = mCΔT = (200.0 g)(4.184)(6.7) ≈ 5610 J released. Moles of water formed = 0.100 mol. ΔH = −5610 J / 0.100 mol = −56,100 J/mol ≈ −56.1 kJ/mol (exothermic).

questions.prompt: Using Hess's law, given (1) C(s) + O₂(g) → CO₂(g), ΔH = −393 kJ and (2) 2 CO(g) + O₂(g) → 2 CO₂(g), ΔH = −566 kJ, what is ΔH for C(s) + ½ O₂(g) → CO(g)?
questions.explanation: Reverse and halve (2): CO₂ → CO + ½ O₂, ΔH = +283 kJ. Add to (1): C + O₂ → CO₂ (−393) plus CO₂ → CO + ½ O₂ (+283). Sum: C + ½ O₂ → CO, ΔH = −393 + 283 = −110 kJ.

questions.prompt: Which of the following processes is predicted to have a positive ΔS_system (increase in entropy)?
questions.explanation: Dissolution disperses a single ordered solid into freely moving aqueous ions — a large positive ΔS. The other choices reduce moles of gas or condense gas to liquid, both of which decrease entropy.

questions.prompt: A reaction has ΔH = +52 kJ/mol and ΔS = +135 J/(mol·K). At what temperature does ΔG = 0 (the equilibrium threshold above which the reaction becomes spontaneous)?
questions.explanation: Using ΔG = ΔH − TΔS = 0: T = ΔH / ΔS = 52,000 J / 135 J·K⁻¹ ≈ 385 K. Below 385 K the endothermic reaction is non-spontaneous; above 385 K the +TΔS term dominates and ΔG turns negative.

questions.prompt: For the equilibrium 2 SO₂(g) + O₂(g) ⇌ 2 SO₃(g) (ΔH < 0), which change will shift the equilibrium toward more product SO₃?
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier: increasing pressure shifts equilibrium toward the side with fewer gas moles. Here, 3 moles of gas reactant → 2 moles of gas product, so the forward direction is favored. Raising T would shift away from product (exothermic); a catalyst doesn't shift equilibrium.

questions.prompt: At a certain temperature, Kc = 50.0 for H₂(g) + I₂(g) ⇌ 2 HI(g). A mixture is prepared with [H₂] = 0.10 M, [I₂] = 0.20 M, and [HI] = 0.30 M. Which statement correctly describes Q and the shift?
choices: Q = 4.5; reaction shifts to form more product (right)
Q = 0.045; reaction shifts to form more reactant (left)
Q = 1.50; reaction shifts to form more reactant (left)
questions.correctText: Q = 4.5; reaction shifts to form more product (right)
questions.explanation: Q = [HI]² / ([H₂][I₂]) = (0.30)² / ((0.10)(0.20)) = 0.09 / 0.02 = 4.5. Q < K, so the reaction proceeds forward to produce more HI until Q rises to 50.0.

questions.prompt: The Ksp of AgCl at 25°C is 1.8 × 10⁻¹⁰. What is the approximate molar solubility of AgCl in pure water?
questions.explanation: AgCl(s) ⇌ Ag⁺ + Cl⁻ gives Ksp = s². s = √(1.8 × 10⁻¹⁰) ≈ 1.34 × 10⁻⁵ M. In a solution containing added Cl⁻ (common-ion effect), solubility would be lower.

questions.prompt: An ICE table is set up for N₂O₄(g) ⇌ 2 NO₂(g) starting with [N₂O₄] = 0.100 M and [NO₂] = 0. At equilibrium, [NO₂] = 0.040 M. What is the equilibrium constant Kc?
questions.explanation: Change in NO₂ is +0.040, so change in N₂O₄ is −0.020 (1:2 stoichiometry). Equilibrium [N₂O₄] = 0.080 M; [NO₂] = 0.040 M. Kc = (0.040)² / 0.080 = 0.0016 / 0.080 = 0.020.

questions.prompt: A saturated solution of PbCl₂ (Ksp = 1.7 × 10⁻⁵) is prepared in 0.10 M NaCl. The common-ion effect predicts that the solubility of PbCl₂ in this solution, compared to pure water:
choices: Increases, because Cl⁻ shifts equilibrium right
Decreases, because added Cl⁻ shifts the equilibrium toward solid PbCl₂
Decreases by a factor equal to Ksp
questions.correctText: Decreases, because added Cl⁻ shifts the equilibrium toward solid PbCl₂
questions.explanation: PbCl₂(s) ⇌ Pb²⁺ + 2 Cl⁻. Adding Cl⁻ (Le Chatelier) shifts equilibrium left, precipitating more PbCl₂. The common-ion effect always reduces solubility compared to the pure-water case.

questions.prompt: A 0.020 M solution of HCl (a strong acid) at 25°C has a pH of:
questions.explanation: HCl dissociates completely, so [H⁺] = 0.020 M. pH = −log(0.020) = −log(2.0 × 10⁻²) = 2 − log 2 ≈ 1.70.

questions.prompt: Which species is the conjugate base of HSO₄⁻ (hydrogen sulfate ion) in Brønsted–Lowry theory?
questions.explanation: A conjugate base is what remains after the acid donates one proton. HSO₄⁻ − H⁺ → SO₄²⁻. (H₂SO₄ is the conjugate acid of HSO₄⁻, going the other way.)

questions.prompt: A buffer is prepared from 0.20 M acetic acid (CH₃COOH, Ka = 1.8 × 10⁻⁵, pKa ≈ 4.74) and 0.20 M sodium acetate. Using the Henderson–Hasselbalch equation, the pH of the buffer is approximately:
questions.explanation: pH = pKa + log([A⁻]/[HA]) = 4.74 + log(0.20/0.20) = 4.74 + 0 = 4.74. A buffer is at maximum capacity (pH ≈ pKa) when [conjugate base] = [acid].

questions.prompt: Which titration curve feature is characteristic of titrating a weak acid (such as acetic acid) with a strong base (NaOH)?
choices: There is a buffer region near pH = pKa, and the equivalence point pH is greater than 7 because of the basic conjugate base in solution.
There is no buffer region at all.
questions.correctText: There is a buffer region near pH = pKa, and the equivalence point pH is greater than 7 because of the basic conjugate base in solution.
questions.explanation: Halfway to equivalence, [HA] = [A⁻] so pH ≈ pKa (buffer region). At equivalence, only the conjugate base A⁻ remains, which hydrolyzes water to give pH > 7. Strong acid + strong base, by contrast, has equivalence at pH = 7.

questions.prompt: What is the key difference between a galvanic (voltaic) cell and an electrolytic cell?
choices: Galvanic cells require an external power source; electrolytic cells do not.
Galvanic cells run a spontaneous redox reaction (ΔG < 0) to generate electrical energy, while electrolytic cells use external electrical energy to drive a non-spontaneous reaction (ΔG > 0).
Galvanic cells use only oxidation; electrolytic cells use only reduction.
Galvanic cells have no salt bridge; electrolytic cells always do.
questions.correctText: Galvanic cells run a spontaneous redox reaction (ΔG < 0) to generate electrical energy, while electrolytic cells use external electrical energy to drive a non-spontaneous reaction (ΔG > 0).
questions.explanation: Galvanic cells (e.g., a battery) convert chemical energy → electrical energy spontaneously. Electrolytic cells (e.g., electroplating, water electrolysis) consume electrical energy to drive a non-spontaneous reaction. In both, oxidation occurs at the anode and reduction at the cathode.

questions.prompt: Using standard reduction potentials E°(Cu²⁺/Cu) = +0.34 V and E°(Zn²⁺/Zn) = −0.76 V, what is the standard cell potential E°cell for a Zn|Zn²⁺ || Cu²⁺|Cu galvanic cell, and is the reaction spontaneous?
questions.explanation: E°cell = E°(cathode) − E°(anode) = (+0.34) − (−0.76) = +1.10 V. A positive E°cell corresponds to ΔG° = −nFE°cell < 0, so the reaction Zn + Cu²⁺ → Zn²⁺ + Cu is spontaneous — the basis of the classic Daniell cell.
